beats.text: A high cross-validated score tells you a model is accurate, but it never tells you why a single prediction came out the way it did.
Explainability serves three distinct masters, and each one asks a different question of the same model.
Debugging is where explanations earn their keep, exposing leakage and shortcut features like snow in the background standing in for husky.
Regulation is no longer hypothetical: the CFPB confirmed in 2023 that opaque models still owe applicants the specific reasons for an adverse action.

visual.label: CFPB ruling on ML adverse-action reasons

beats.text: The EU AI Act takes force from 2024, with high-risk obligations for credit scoring and medical devices phasing in through 2027.
Treat every method here with care: an explanation is a model of a model, a post-hoc approximation that can be unstable, unfaithful, or even manipulated.
Explanations split first along scope: a global one describes the whole model, a local one describes a single prediction.
Global says income is the most important feature on average; local says this applicant was denied chiefly for three recent late payments.
A feature can be globally unimportant yet decisive for one row, and globally important yet irrelevant for another.
Averaging local explanations recovers a global one, but you cannot run that inference backwards to any single applicant.
A second axis is model access: agnostic methods only call predict, while model-specific methods read internal structure for speed and fidelity.
Match scope to the decision: a board deploying a fraud model wants the global picture, a customer disputing a blocked card wants the local one.

visual.sub: Wrong scope is the common error

beats.text: The cheapest global tool asks a blunt question: if I shuffle a feature's column, how much worse does the model get?

visual.sub: Break the feature, measure the drop

beats.text: Importance is the drop in score caused by breaking the link between a feature and the target, averaged over K shuffles.

visual.tex: \mathrm{Imp}_j = s(\hat{f}, X, y) - \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} s(\hat{f}, X^{(\pi_k, j)}, y)
visual.note: score drop from permuting column j

beats.text: On a toy model that truly uses x0 strongly and x1 mildly while ignoring x2 and x3, the importance ranking recovers exactly that reliance.
Correlated features split and hide each other's importance: shuffle one and the model leans on its twin, so both look unimportant.
Partial dependence answers shape: fix a feature to a value for every row, average the predictions, and sweep the value across its range.

visual.note: average effect of feature j alone

beats.text: ICE plots one line per row instead of averaging; when those lines fan out and cross, the flat-looking average is hiding subgroups.
LIME explains one prediction by approximating the black box with a simple model in a small neighbourhood around that point.
Any wiggly decision surface looks roughly linear if you zoom in far enough, so a straight line can stand in locally.
The recipe: perturb around x, query the model, weight each sample by proximity, then fit a sparse linear surrogate.

visual.note: RBF proximity weight on perturbed sample z

beats.text: The objective trades global accuracy for local interpretability: the simplest surrogate that stays faithful right around x.

visual.note: local fidelity plus a simplicity penalty

beats.text: The neighbourhood width sigma is a free knob, and it is exactly LIME's weak spot: the explanation can swing with it.
Re-running with a new seed or a different bandwidth can perturb the weights, so two LIME runs can disagree on the same instance.
SHAP is the most-used method in the field because it rests on the one thing everything else lacks: a uniqueness theorem.
Cast the prediction as a coalition payout and the features as players, and the Shapley value is the only fair way to split it.

visual.note: average marginal contribution over all orderings

beats.text: The axiom that matters for an audit is efficiency: the attributions and the base value add up to exactly the prediction.

visual.note: base value plus signed feature pushes

beats.text: With a base value of 0.30 and a prediction of 0.82, efficiency forces the feature pushes to total exactly 0.52.
Computing the values exactly costs two to the power of the feature count, so TreeSHAP and KernelSHAP make it tractable.
On a toy loan score, dragging recent late payments up grows one red arrow until it alone flips the decision to denial.
